content: Hey Saturday, I'm finally tackling my garage this weekend. It's been a disaster zone for like 3 years — just boxes everywhere, old tools I don't use, Christmas decorations mixed in with summer stuff. I don't even know where to start. Should I just pull everything out first or try to organize it in sections?

Great timing to tackle it! The pull-everything-out method is actually best — it forces you to evaluate every single item and see what you're really working with. Plus you get a clean slate to reorganize strategically. Start by clearing one section at a time (maybe left wall, then right wall, then back). As items come out, sort them into piles: keep, sell/donate, and trash. This way you'll see exactly how much space you have available once you remove the dead weight. How many square feet is your garage, roughly?

It's a standard two-car garage, so probably about 400-450 sq feet. The issue is I've got my car taking up one spot, and I'd like to keep it that way, but the other half is just completely packed. I've got a workbench that I use maybe once every two months, some old bikes, and honestly a lot of stuff that belonged to my ex that I never got around to sorting through.

Okay, so you're really working with about 200 square feet of usable space since the car takes up half. That's actually manageable. First priority: go through that ex's stuff immediately. It's probably the biggest space-taker and emotional blocker. Sell, donate, or throw out anything that's not genuinely useful to you anymore. That could free up 30-50 square feet right there. For your workbench — if you're only using it biweekly, consider whether a smaller folding workbench would work instead, or if it could move to a corner instead of taking up prime real estate. What kind of work do you actually do on it?

I mostly do light woodworking projects and basic maintenance — fixing bikes, small furniture repairs. The current workbench is pretty solid, takes up maybe 4x2 feet. And yeah, you're right about the ex's stuff — it's probably time to just let it go. I think there are some old clothes, tools that aren't mine, and some furniture that's just been sitting there. Should I prioritize clearing that first before making any structural decisions?

Absolutely, clear that out first — it's a psychological win and gives you accurate numbers on what space you actually need for your own stuff. Once that's gone, you'll have a clearer picture. For your woodworking setup, the 4x2 workbench is reasonable if you're using it regularly enough. I'd suggest mounting a pegboard on the wall above it (3-4 feet high) for tools instead of having them take up bench or shelf space. That vertical thinking is key in a 200 sq ft space. The bikes — how many do you have, and do you actually ride them all regularly?

Oh man, the bikes are a problem. I have five bikes total, but I only regularly ride two of them. Three are pretty much dead weight — old mountain bikes from college that don't even fit me properly anymore. One has a flat tire and I've been meaning to fix it for two years. I'm definitely selling those or donating them. That's probably another 20 square feet or so if I count the wall space they're taking up.

Five bikes is excessive if you're only using two — good call letting those three go. Those could be quick cash on Facebook Marketplace or Craigslist, especially if you bundle them. Even non-functional ones often appeal to people who flip bikes. Once you're down to two bikes, consider a wall-mounted bike rack system (the kind that hangs them vertically) — takes almost no floor space and keeps them out of the way. You could mount those above where your car isn't parked. So far you're looking at freeing up 50-70 square feet just from the ex's stuff and extra bikes. What about the Christmas decorations — do you actually use them every year?

Yeah, I use the decorations every December, but they're currently just scattered in random boxes. Some of it's broken — I think there's a light strand that doesn't work, and a few ornaments got damaged last year. I should probably consolidate it all into maybe two clear storage boxes instead of five or six. And I need to figure out where to store seasonal stuff so it's not mixed in with tools.

Perfect, consolidating seasonal items is smart. Toss the broken lights and damaged ornaments — if you haven't fixed them by now, you won't. Get two clear 50-quart storage bins (like the Rubbermaid ones) labeled clearly. For seasonal storage strategy, create a vertical storage zone — maybe a tall shelving unit along one wall, with seasonal stuff on the upper shelves where you don't need frequent access. Keep your everyday tools at arm height on the workbench area. This separation makes a huge difference. Speaking of storage — do you have any wall space you could utilize? Most garages have wasted vertical real estate. Are your walls mostly open or do you have existing shelving?

One wall has an old wooden shelving unit that's pretty rickety — honestly it looks like it might collapse if I put anything heavy on it. The other walls are pretty bare except for a few hooks. So I'm thinking I should probably replace that sketchy shelving with something more sturdy. How much space should I plan for tool storage specifically? I don't have a huge tool collection, just basics — hammer, screwdrivers, socket set, drill, that kind of thing.

Definitely get rid of that rickety shelving unit — it's a liability and taking up space. For your tool collection, a good pegboard setup (4x8 feet is standard) would be ideal mounted above your workbench. That costs $30-50 and lets you see everything at a glance. Alternatively, wall-mounted magnetic strips for screwdrivers and a socket organizer work too. For a basic toolkit like yours, you really only need 3-4 linear feet of organized storage. Consider building a 2x4 frame shelving unit yourself or buying a metal industrial shelving set ($100-150) — those are way sturdier than wood and great for garages. How about storage for things like garden tools, ladders, that kind of seasonal stuff?

I have a ladder, a couple rakes, and some shovels. Garden hose too. Those things honestly get in the way more than anything. Could I mount them vertically on the wall instead of leaning them everywhere? And should I think about overhead storage for things I use rarely, like camping gear or those Christmas boxes once they're organized?

Absolutely — vertical wall storage is your best friend. Get wall-mounted hooks or a ladder rack ($20-40) to hang the ladder horizontally, and use pegboard hooks for rakes and shovels. Super space-efficient. For overhead storage, ceiling-mounted racks are excellent for seasonal stuff like camping gear and Christmas boxes. Just make sure they're properly installed into the joists — you want something that won't fail with weight. A typical ceiling rack system costs $100-200 and can hold 300+ pounds. Keep overhead storage for things you access 2-3 times per year max, not your regular tools or current projects. How tall are your ceilings? That matters for safety and clearance above your car.

Standard garage ceilings, I'd say around 8.5 to 9 feet. So there's definitely room for overhead racks. Okay, I'm getting a clearer picture now. Let me ask about the actual layout — should I arrange my workbench against a wall or in the corner? And where should my car be positioned to maximize the open side of the garage?

Good question. I'd recommend putting your workbench in a corner (like back-left or back-right) so it's out of the main traffic flow and you have clear space to work without bumping into things. This also keeps tools and projects away from the car entrance area. Position your car predictably — the side that's not blocked by the workbench. This keeps your main entry/exit lane clear and prevents you from having to move the car to access storage. Leave at least 3-4 feet of working space in front of the car, and keep your wall-mounted tools, pegboard, and shelving on the walls behind and beside the workbench. Have you thought about where you'd put that new shelving unit?

The wall opposite where I'll put the workbench would work — that way it's visible when I pull in, and I can access it without moving the car. I'm also thinking about putting the ceiling racks above the car's parking area since that's dead space I'm not using anyway. Does that make sense? And should I worry about clearance for getting the car in and out?

Perfect layout thinking — ceiling racks above the car space is ideal since you're not using that overhead area. Just be strategic about what you store up there. Leave at least 12-18 inches of clearance above the highest part of your car (roof rack if you have one, plus a foot of buffer). Measure your car height with any roof equipment included, then mount racks at that clearance level. For accessing items, use lower-strength racks or only store lightweight, squishy items like camping tents and Christmas decorations up there. Heavy tools belong on wall-mounted shelving at eye level. What about creating a dedicated zone for the things you frequently access versus seasonal storage?

That makes sense. So like, my current project materials, drill, socket set, and frequently-used hand tools in the workbench area, and seasonal stuff up top and in bins on shelves. One more thing — I've been wondering about the floor space. Should I get some kind of flooring treatment, or is concrete okay as-is? It's pretty stained, and I'm worried about moisture in the winter.

Good thinking about moisture — that's a real issue in garages. Before investing in flooring, address the moisture problem. Get a dehumidifier running on cold/damp days, and make sure your garage door seals properly and gutters drain away from the foundation. For flooring itself, stained concrete is fine for basic storage and working. If you want to improve it, epoxy coating ($300-600 for DIY) looks nice and seals the concrete. Interlocking floor mats ($200-400 for partial coverage) are another option — they're softer to stand on and protect against spills. But honestly, with your budget of creating organized storage, I'd skip fancy flooring for now and focus on ventilation and organization. Have you decided on a rough budget for the shelving and storage systems?

I'm thinking around $400-500 total for storage stuff — new shelving unit, pegboard, ceiling rack system, wall hooks, and those clear storage bins. Does that seem reasonable? And should I tackle this whole project in one weekend, or spread it out?

Your budget is realistic for quality items. Breaking it down: industrial shelving ($150-200), pegboard setup with tools ($60-80), ceiling racks ($100-150), bins and hooks ($60-100). You'll stay in budget. As for timeline, I'd split it into two weekends. Weekend one: completely clear everything out, sort ruthlessly, sell/donate bikes and ex's items, identify what's trash. That's physically exhausting and emotionally demanding. Weekend two: install shelving and racks, organize what you're keeping into zones. Trying to do it all at once is overwhelming and leads to bad decisions. Plus you'll know exactly what you have between weekends, so you can buy the right storage sizes. Does that work with your schedule?

Yeah, that two-weekend approach makes more sense. Actually, quick question — when I'm sorting through stuff, how do I decide what to keep versus sell? Like, I feel guilty about getting rid of things sometimes, even if I don't use them. What's a good rule of thumb?

Great question, because guilt is why garages get cluttered. Use the 'one-year rule' — if you haven't used something in a year, you probably won't. There are exceptions (seasonal items, emergency tools), but be honest. Ask yourself: would I buy this again today? If the answer is no, sell or donate it. For the ex's stuff specifically, that's easier — it's not yours, so no attachment needed. Be ruthless. For tools you're unsure about, keep only if you've used it in the last 12 months or have a specific project planned. A hammer you haven't touched in three years? Gone. That camping tent you used twice? Sell it unless you're genuinely planning a trip. Don't keep 'just in case' items — they're why you have no space. How are you feeling about letting go of things?

Honestly, pretty good about most of it. The bikes I'm ready to let go of, the ex's stuff definitely goes. I think the hardest part will be the random boxes of stuff I don't even remember buying — like I know there are old electronics, cords, things like that. Should I try to recycle those, or just trash them?

Old electronics and cords are valuable — don't trash them. Old computer cables, phone chargers, USB drives? Someone will buy those cheaply on eBay or Facebook Marketplace, or donate to a tech recycling program if they're broken. Even ancient stuff like old routers or external hard drives have resale value for parts or repair. Set aside a 'tech/cord' bin and photograph it for online sale — you might get $20-50 for a lot of it. It's also more responsible than landfill. Just be aware that cords take up mental space even if they're small, so only keep ones you actually use. Like, how many charging cables do you genuinely need? If you have four phone chargers for phones you don't own anymore, trash those. Be selective about what you save 'just in case.'

Good point. Okay so I'm planning to start this Saturday. Before I do, should I rent a dumpster or can I just haul stuff to my car and take it to the dump in multiple trips? Also, do you have any tips for listing those bikes online — what price range should I ask?

Skip the dumpster rental for this — too expensive for a one-off project. You can handle it with car trips. Trash goes to the landfill, recyclables go to your recycling center, and items to sell/donate need separate handling. Plan for maybe 3-4 trips over the next two weeks. For the bikes, check eBay sold listings and Facebook Marketplace in your area to see what similar models are going for. Used mountain bikes typically go for $50-150 depending on condition and brand. If yours are beat up, list them at $40-75 each or bundle all three for $100-150 to move them faster. Take clear photos from multiple angles, note any damage honestly, and mention that some need tune-ups. Be prepared to negotiate down — people always want deals. What brands are your old bikes, and do you remember how old they are?

I think they're a mix of Huffy and some generic department store bikes from like 2010-2012 era. So they're pretty old and not high-end brands. That probably means I should price them lower, right? I just want them gone honestly, so even if I only get $20-30 each, that's better than letting them sit there for another year.

Honestly, yeah — Huffy and department store bikes are going to be $25-50 each at best. Pricing them aggressively at $20-40 per bike or $60-100 for all three will move them fast. Include 'free pickup' and you'll get interest immediately. The goal is to free up space and mental energy, not maximize profit. You could also check if local bike shops do donation pickups — some do, which saves you the hassle of dealing with buyers. That might honestly be worth it for you if the time investment isn't worth the $60-100 you'd make. Have you thought about what you're going to do with the money if you do sell them? Sometimes having a small reward (like buying something functional for the garage) helps motivation.

That's actually a cool idea. I could put any sale money toward the storage project — like, if I sell those bikes for $75 and the ex's stuff for another $100 or so, that could cover part of the shelving cost. It'd feel good to fund the organization project with items I'm getting rid of. Speaking of which, what should I do with stuff that's broken but might be fixable? Like, should I even bother trying to repair things before selling?

Don't invest time or money repairing things you're trying to sell — just be honest about the condition. List a bike as 'needs tune-up, cheap project' instead of trying to fix the flat tire. People who buy things cheap know they're getting projects. You'll spend three hours fixing a tire and charging, and still sell it for $25. Not worth it. The exception is if it's a quick 5-minute fix (like pumping up that tire you mentioned). For truly broken items, be honest in listings — 'doesn't work, selling for parts' — and price accordingly. Buyers understand and expect it. Your time is worth more than the $10-15 difference between 'broken' and 'fixed' pricing. Focus on getting things out, not perfecting them. Speaking of broken stuff, what's in those random boxes besides electronics? Any furniture or things your ex might want back?

Mostly just miscellaneous stuff, honestly. There might be some old kitchen gadgets that came from her place, maybe a small bookshelf? I'm not in contact with her anymore, so it's not like I can ask. I think I should just assume it's mine to do with what I want at this point, but I don't want any of it. The sooner it's gone, the better.

Absolutely — after this much time, it's fair to treat it as yours to dispose of. Donate the bookshelf to Goodwill (they'll pick up larger items if you schedule it), post the kitchen gadgets in a 'free stuff' box on Facebook Marketplace, or just trash what nobody wants. The psychological benefit of clearing her stuff out will be worth way more than whatever small amount you'd make selling it. It's part of moving forward, honestly. Plus you'll feel so much lighter once it's gone. This might be the most important psychological step of the whole project. Once you're planning to clear it this weekend, how will you handle the emotional side of it? Sometimes throwing out reminders of a relationship is harder than people expect.

You know, I think I'm actually ready for it. We've been broken up for like three years, and keeping her stuff just feels like I'm stuck. Clearing it out feels like closure, if that sounds dramatic. So yeah, I'm looking forward to it more than I thought I would be. Okay, last planning question — should I take before/after photos? Seems silly but part of me wants documentation.

Not silly at all — take photos! Before/after pics are super motivating, and honestly it's fun to look back on what you accomplished. Take a few shots from different angles of the chaos before you start, then the same angles after organizing. You'll be amazed at the transformation and it's a nice reminder of why you did all this work when you get lazy about maintaining it. Plus, if you ever sell the house, a clean garage is a selling point. You'll want visual proof of your effort. The whole project is going to feel amazing once it's done. So to recap your plan: weekend one is full clear-out and sorting, sell/donate the major items, weekend two is installing storage systems and organizing. You've got a solid $400-500 budget and a good layout plan. Feeling ready to start Saturday?

Yeah, I'm feeling good about it. One last thing I forgot to ask — should I wear a mask or anything while I'm clearing things out? I'm worried about dust, especially if there's been stuff sitting in boxes for years.

Smart thinking — yes, definitely wear a dust mask and consider safety glasses. Old boxes can have dust, mold spores, and spider webs that you don't want inhaling. Grab a box of N95 masks ($10-15) from any hardware store or pharmacy. Also wear work gloves because you never know what's been sitting in those boxes — rusty nails, broken glass, sharp edges, or gross stuff from mice if you've had any. Long sleeves and closed-toe shoes too. Open the garage door and maybe a window for ventilation, or run a fan to push dust outside. If you find any obvious mold or smell anything funky, stop and check for moisture issues before continuing. These precautions make the work way less unpleasant. Anything else you want to nail down before you start Saturday?

I think that covers it. Last thing — what should I do if I start the project and realize I need to buy storage stuff mid-way? Should I hold off on clearing until I have everything, or clear first and then install the storage?

Clear first, then buy and install storage. Here's why: you don't know exactly how much space you'll have freed up until you actually pull everything out. You might think you need a 5-shelf unit but end up needing a 3-shelf. The final layout might be slightly different than you planned. So weekend one, do the brutal clearing, sorting, and donating. Take measurements of your clean walls, count what you're actually keeping, and note the sizes and quantities. Then you can shop smart for exactly what you need — no overbuying or undershooting. Plus, it's way easier to install shelving in an empty garage than an empty garage. You'll have space to move around and stage your storage systems. Then weekend two, with everything measured and cleared, you install and organize. Does that order make sense?

Perfect, that makes total sense. So basically weekend one is brutal honesty about what I own and getting it all out, then weekend two I know my measurements and can shop smart before installing. I feel like I have a solid plan now. Should I schedule the Goodwill or donation pickup for the middle of the week after weekend one?

Exactly — you've got the plan solid. Yes, schedule any large donations (like that bookshelf or ex's furniture) for the middle of the week. Goodwill and most nonprofits do free pickup if you schedule a few days ahead. Have your donations sorted and ready by Tuesday/Wednesday so they can come pick up. That clears the space you're using as a 'holding zone' before weekend two. For smaller items like clothes or kitchen stuff, you could donate to Goodwill and drop them off yourself, or list as free stuff on Facebook. The goal is that by Friday night before weekend two, your garage is empty except for items you're definitely keeping. Then Saturday morning you wake up to a blank slate, measure everything carefully, and start your shopping list. You've got this — this garage is going to look amazing in two weeks. Good luck this Saturday!

Thanks so much, Saturday! This was really helpful. I feel way less overwhelmed now that I have an actual plan. I'll report back after weekend one with an update on what I got rid of and how much space I freed up. Hopefully the before/after pics will be dramatic!

Please do! I'd love to hear how it goes. Seriously, the fact that you're tackling this is huge — most people just live with garage chaos forever. The fact that you're being intentional about layout, vertical storage, and actually using the space is going to make a real difference. Remember: be ruthless with the sorting, use that one-year rule, and don't second-guess your decisions. Once something is listed for sale or going to Goodwill, let it go mentally. The closure from clearing your ex's stuff plus the satisfaction of an organized workspace is going to feel amazing. Enjoy the process, be safe with the dust and heavy lifting, and come back and tell me how many bikes sold and what you found in those mystery boxes. You've got a solid two-week project ahead — excited for you!
